class_schedule.topic: The Landscape of Emerging Technology UX
learning_objectives: Survey the UX challenges of AR, VR, voice, AI, and IoT interfaces
Identify interaction paradigms unique to spatial, conversational, and ambient computing
Evaluate emerging technology maturity using the Gartner Hype Cycle framework
readings: Designing Across Senses by Christine Park & John Alderman, Ch. 1-3
The Design of Future Things by Don Norman, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Emerging technologies demand new interaction paradigms. Screen-based design principles do not directly transfer to spatial, conversational, or ambient computing environments.
key_concepts: Spatial computing: AR and VR interaction in 3D space
Conversational UI: voice assistants and chatbots
Ambient computing: IoT, smart environments, and calm technology
AI-mediated interfaces: recommendation engines, generative AI, adaptive systems
The challenge of designing without screens or with augmented screens
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on comparison of three interaction paradigms: completing the same task via a mobile app, a voice assistant, and an AR prototype

review.value: Interfaces exist in 3D space around the user — interactions use gaze, gesture, and voice rather than touch on a flat screen. Depth, spatial audio, and physical comfort become design concerns.
Ambient interfaces that inform without demanding attention — using peripheral awareness (LED colors, gentle sounds) rather than requiring active screen interaction

learning_objectives: Apply spatial design principles for AR overlays on the real world
Design AR interactions that respect physical context and user safety
Address AR-specific challenges: occlusion, lighting, field of view
readings: AR/VR Design Best Practices by Google ARCore team
Spatial Computing by Avi Bar-Zeev, selected chapters

lecture_notes.introduction: AR design overlays digital content onto the physical world. Unlike screen design, AR must account for varying physical environments, lighting conditions, user movement, and the critical need to maintain awareness of the real world.
key_concepts: World-locked vs. screen-locked AR content placement
Spatial anchoring: content that stays in place as users move
Field of view limitations and content placement strategies
Occlusion handling: digital content interacting with physical objects
Safety considerations: maintaining peripheral vision and environmental awareness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing and testing an AR navigation prototype using SparkAR or Reality Composer — placing directional arrows in physical space

review.value: AR elements anchored to a physical location — they stay in place as the user moves, like a virtual label on a physical object. Requires accurate spatial tracking.
AR must never obscure critical real-world information — crosswalks, obstacles, other people. Design guidelines require maintaining peripheral vision and providing exit-AR at all times.

learning_objectives: Design voice interactions following conversational design principles
Write dialog flows that handle errors, disambiguation, and conversation repair
Address voice-specific challenges: discoverability, trust, and privacy
readings: Conversational Design by Erika Hall, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Voice interfaces have no visual affordances — users cannot see what is possible. Conversational design must guide users through available actions using dialog structure, prompts, and graceful error recovery.
key_concepts: Conversational design principles: cooperative, turn-taking, contextual
Dialog flow design: intents, entities, contexts, and fallbacks
Error handling: no-match, no-input, disambiguation, conversation repair
Persona design: voice character, tone, vocabulary matching brand
Multimodal design: voice plus screen experiences
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a complete voice interaction for ordering coffee — mapping happy path, error paths, and edge cases in a dialog flow diagram
setup_requirements: Dialog flow diagramming tool (Voiceflow or Miro)

review.value: Users cannot see what a voice interface can do — unlike visual UI where buttons show available actions. Solutions: progressive disclosure, implicit teaching, and conversation-structured guidance.
When the system misunderstands, it must recover gracefully: offering options rather than asking the user to repeat identically.

learning_objectives: Design interfaces for AI-powered features: recommendations, predictions, generation
Build user trust through transparency, explainability, and control
Address ethical considerations in AI-mediated user experiences
readings: Google PAIR Guidelines for Human-AI Interaction

lecture_notes.introduction: AI is increasingly mediating user experiences — recommending content, predicting behavior, generating text and images. Designing AI interfaces requires addressing trust, transparency, control, and the inevitable errors.
key_concepts: Transparency: showing users how AI decisions are made
Confidence indicators: communicating AI certainty levels to users
User control: always allowing override of AI suggestions
Error communication: what to do when AI gets it wrong
Ethical AI UX: bias awareness, consent, data privacy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing the interface for an AI-powered email drafting feature — handling confidence levels, user editing, and communicating when the AI is uncertain

review.value: Users trust AI more when they understand why it made a recommendation — showing reasoning builds more trust than an unexplained list.
AI will be wrong. Design must make errors visible, easily correctable, and non-destructive. Always provide an easy way to reject AI suggestions and provide feedback.

learning_objectives: Design experiences that span multiple connected devices seamlessly
Apply calm technology principles to IoT interfaces
Address IoT-specific challenges: onboarding, connectivity, and status communication
readings: Designing Connected Products by Rowland et al., Ch. 6-8
Calm Technology by Amber Case, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: IoT experiences span multiple devices, touchpoints, and time. The user journey might start on a phone, continue on a smart display, and be monitored via notifications — designing this holistic experience is a new frontier for UX.
key_concepts: Cross-device journey mapping: how experiences flow between devices
Device-appropriate interaction: phone for setup, display for glancing, voice for hands-free
IoT onboarding: pairing, Wi-Fi setup, account creation — the first-run cliff
Status communication: how a device tells you it is working when there is no screen
Offline resilience: IoT devices must work when the cloud is unreachable
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing the complete user journey for a smart home lighting system — from unboxing through daily use across phone app, voice assistant, and physical switch
setup_requirements: State diagram tool for device behavior

review.value: Users do not think in devices — they think in tasks. The UX must ensure seamless handoff between devices while all stay in sync.
Smart device setup is the number one failure point — Wi-Fi pairing, account creation, firmware updates. Each step loses users. Design for the least technical user.

questions.question: Compare the primary interaction models for AR (smartphone-based) and VR (headset-based). What are the key design constraints unique to each, and what principle do they share?
questions.answer: AR (smartphone): Primary interaction through touch on a 2D screen while viewing 3D content through the camera. Key constraints: limited field of view, need to hold the device steady, real-world environment variability, and maintaining safety awareness. VR (headset): Primary interaction through hand controllers or hand tracking in 360-degree space. Key constraints: no external visual reference (causing motion sickness), text readability at distance, physical fatigue from extended arm gestures. Shared principle: both require spatial awareness in design — content must be placed considering the user's physical space, comfort zones, and natural body movement ranges.

questions.question: A VR application has users reading long text passages displayed on a flat panel 5 meters away with small font. Identify the usability problems and propose fixes.
questions.answer: Problems: (1) 5 meters is too far — comfortable VR reading distance is 1-2 meters. (2) Long text passages cause eye strain due to the vergence-accommodation conflict in current headsets. (3) Flat panels at distance lack spatial context. Fixes: Move the panel to 1.2-1.5 meters, curve it slightly for consistent focal distance, break text into shorter paginated chunks, use minimum 0.5 degrees visual arc text size, and provide audio narration as an alternative.

rubric.key: Identifies distance problem with correct range
Identifies eye strain from long reading in VR
Proposes specific fixes including distance and text size

questions.question: Design a voice interaction flow for a user asking a smart speaker to set a reminder. Include the happy path and at least two error recovery paths.
questions.answer: Happy path: User: 'Set a reminder for tomorrow at 3pm to call the dentist.' System: 'I will remind you tomorrow at 3pm to call the dentist. Is that right?' User: 'Yes.' System: 'Reminder set.' Error path 1 — Ambiguous time: User: 'Remind me to call the dentist tomorrow.' System: 'What time should I remind you?' User: '3pm.' System: 'Reminder set for tomorrow at 3pm.' Error path 2 — Misrecognition: User: 'Set a reminder to call the dentist.' System: 'I heard call the Dennis. Did you mean call the dentist?' User: 'Yes.' System: 'When should I remind you?' Key principles: always confirm before committing, offer correction opportunities, ask for missing information one piece at a time.

rubric.key: Error path for misrecognition with repair

questions.question: An AI-powered email tool auto-generates reply suggestions. Users report they do not trust the suggestions. Propose three design changes to improve trust.
questions.answer: Three changes: (1) Contextual explanation — show a tooltip explaining the basis: 'Based on your previous replies to this sender' or 'Matching the formal tone of the original email.' This makes reasoning transparent. (2) Confidence indicator — display confidence level for each suggestion so users can calibrate review effort. (3) Easy editing with diff — when a user selects a suggestion, highlight AI-generated portions so they can quickly identify and edit the AI's contribution versus their own additions.

rubric.key: User control and editing visibility feature

major_assignments.title: Emerging Technology UX Concept
major_assignments.description: Design a UX concept for an emerging technology platform (AR, VR, voice, AI, or IoT). Deliver a complete interaction model, prototyped key flows, and user testing findings. The concept must address a real user need that cannot be adequately served by existing screen-based interfaces.
objectives: Identify a genuine user need that benefits from emerging technology
Design an interaction model appropriate for the chosen technology
Prototype key interactions using appropriate tools
Test with users and iterate based on findings
deliverables: Concept document: user need, technology choice rationale, and interaction model
Interaction flow diagrams for 3 key user journeys
Prototype of the primary interaction (tool appropriate to technology)
Design guidelines document for the chosen technology interaction patterns

criteria: Genuine user need identified that benefits from the technology
Technology choice is justified over screen-based alternatives
Interaction model is appropriate for the technology constraints
Concept is feasible with current or near-term technology
Interaction patterns are appropriate for the technology
Edge cases and error states are addressed
Accessibility and safety considerations included
Design respects unique constraints of the chosen technology
Prototype is testable and demonstrates key interactions
Testing reveals genuine insights about the technology interaction
Findings well-documented with iteration evidence
Testing methodology appropriate for the technology
Design guidelines are generalizable beyond this specific project
Documentation is professional and thorough
Rationale connects design decisions to user needs and technology constraints
Presentation effectively communicates the concept
Compelling concept addressing a real need with appropriate technology
Well-designed interaction model respecting technology constraints
Testable prototype with meaningful user testing findings
Professional documentation with generalizable design guidelines

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on emerging technology interaction paradigms and design principles

technology_labs.description: Weekly labs prototyping interactions for AR, VR, voice, AI, and IoT

emerging_tech_project.description: Emerging technology UX concept with prototype and user testing

technology_critiques.description: Weekly analysis of emerging technology products and their UX approaches

final_presentation.description: Presentation of emerging technology concept to industry professionals

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions require specialized equipment and cannot be replicated independently. Attendance is mandatory for all labs. More than 2 lab absences affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Labs are due weekly. Late work penalized 10% per day. The final project has a firm deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be collaborative for equipment sharing. All design work and prototypes must be individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All concepts and designs must be original. Using existing AR/VR templates without significant modification is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to VR headset (available in lab), AR-capable smartphone, voice assistant device or simulator, Figma, and prototyping tools appropriate to chosen technology.
